extra.reasoning:
This is a state machine. Property G(opted_out -> not sent): in every reachable state, opted_out true implies sent false. I examined every transition that can set sent:=true while opted_out is true. Only [t_resume_glitch] (on send_attempt if opted_out and ticks>=2: sent:=true) sets sent true under opted_out, so it is the violation vector. To enable it I must reach opted_out=true with ticks>=2, then issue send_attempt.

Execution trace from initial state (opted_out=false, sent=false, ticks=0):
1. optout -> [t_optout] (guard true): opted_out=true, sent=false, ticks=0.
2. tick -> [t_tick] (guard true): ticks 0<2 so ticks=1, sent=false. State: opted_out=true, sent=false, ticks=1.
3. tick -> [t_tick]: ticks 1<2 so ticks=2, sent=false. State: opted_out=true, sent=false, ticks=2.
4. send_attempt: guards now enabled are [t_send_blocked] (opted_out true) and [t_resume_glitch] (opted_out true and ticks=2>=2). [t_resume_glitch] sets sent:=true. Resulting state: opted_out=true, sent=true, ticks=2.

This state has opted_out=true and sent=true, violating opted_out -> not sent. The path is 4 events, within the 6-event bound. Because the resume_glitch transition is enabled and can fire on this reachable execution, the safety property is violated regardless of any tie-break with t_send_blocked; for a "G must hold on every reachable execution" property it suffices that some enabled transition reaches a violating state.
